message.content:
Implement the following plan:

# Context
User has four Google Scholar export XML files (MISQ, JMIS, ISR, MS) that capture bibliographic metadata scraped from web searches. They want a single Markdown summary that preserves every citation and organizes them by research topic (per their preference for theme-based grouping).

# Approach
1. Parse each Excel-style XML file (e.g., `Scholar Export` worksheet) to extract every row’s Title, Authors, Journal, Year, Abstract/Notes, DOI/Open access URL, and PDF URL so no paper is missed.
2. Review titles/abstract notes to identify a small set of consistent research directions (e.g., Website Experience & Design, AI/Automation-Driven Systems, Consumer/Trust Signals, Visual/Multimedia Interactions). Record which keywords or concepts trigger each category so the classification logic can be repeated if more exports arrive.
3. Draft a Markdown summary (`organized-scholar-entries.md` in the project folder) with a top-level heading for each topic. Under each heading, list every relevant citation as a bullet containing the title (linked to DOI/PDF if available), authors, journal/year, and any salient notes (e.g., abstract highlights). Maintain the original count of entries by cross-checking totals against each XML file.
4. Double-check that no rows were skipped (compare row totals across files and the Markdown entry counts) and that every provided metadata field is surfaced in the summary.

# Files to Modify
- `/Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/scholar-export-2026-03-20 MISQ.xml` (read-only data source)
- `/Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/scholar-export-2026-03-20 JMIS.xml` (read-only data source)
- `/Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/scholar-export-2026-03-20 ISR.xml` (read-only data source)
- `/Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/scholar-export-2026-03-20 MS.xml` (read-only data source)
- `/Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/organized-scholar-entries.md` (new summary output)

# Verification
- Confirm the number of clipped citations in the Markdown file matches the total number of rows across the four XML exports.
- Ensure each entry includes at least title, authors, journal/year, and a hyperlink to the DOI or PDF URL.
- Validate that every topic heading contains at least one paper, and no citation appears under the wrong theme (spot-check by matching keywords to categories).

If you need specific details from before exiting plan mode (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /Users/my/.claude/projects/-Users-my/9301242d-edf4-4a9c-ad1d-99f7e035cd8a.jsonl

input.content:
# Context
User has four Google Scholar export XML files (MISQ, JMIS, ISR, MS) that capture bibliographic metadata scraped from web searches. They want a single Markdown summary that preserves every citation and organizes them by research topic (theme-based grouping).

# Goal
Produce `/Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/organized-scholar-entries.md` containing every citation from the four XML exports, grouped under clear research-topic headings, with each entry including:
- Title (linked to DOI or PDF when available)
- Authors
- Journal / Year
- Any abstract/notes or salient highlights
- DOI / open-access URL / PDF URL surfaced explicitly

Also produce a simple classification ruleset (keywords -> topic) saved inside this plan file for repeatability.

# Files (read-only sources)
- /Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/scholar-export-2026-03-20 MISQ.xml
- /Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/scholar-export-2026-03-20 JMIS.xml
- /Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/scholar-export-2026-03-20 ISR.xml
- /Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/scholar-export-2026-03-20 MS.xml

# Output (to create)
- /Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/organized-scholar-entries.md (Markdown summary)

# Constraints and non-negotiables
- Do NOT modify the source XML files (read-only).
- Preserve every row unless the user requests deduplication (see question below).
- Maintain the total-entry count and report it in the Markdown header.
- All generated outputs and verification logs will be stored in the project folder (no changes to `papers/`).

# Proposed approach (implementation plan)
1. Parse the four XML files as spreadsheet-style XML (Excel "XML Spreadsheet 2003" format that Google Scholar provides). For each file, extract rows from the worksheet (expected worksheet name: "Scholar Export" or the first worksheet) and capture these fields (if present): Title, Authors, Journal, Year, Abstract/Notes, DOI/Open access URL, PDF URL, and any other columns.

2. Normalize metadata for each row:
   - Trim whitespace and unify whitespace/newlines in abstract/notes.
   - Normalize authors as provided (do not reformat names unless requested).
   - Prefer DOI link when present; otherwise use PDF URL or open-access URL.

3. (Optional) Deduplication: if requested, deduplicate across the four files using DOI when present, else normalized title string. By default, keep all rows and report duplicates separately.

4. Topic classification: two options (choose by user):
   A. Use the user's suggested topic set (recommended):
      - Website Experience & Design
      - AI / Automation-Driven Systems
      - Consumer / Trust Signals
      - Visual / Multimedia Interactions
      For each topic, classification triggers are keyword matches in Title + Abstract/Notes. I will produce a keyword list for each topic (examples below) and apply a simple multi-label assignment (a paper can appear under multiple topics). If a paper matches no keywords, it will go under "Other / Uncategorized".

   B. Auto-extract topics: use unsupervised clustering on title/abstract text (TF-IDF + Agglomerative or KMeans) to produce 4-6 coherent clusters, then label them manually. This is more flexible but harder to reproduce by simple keyword rules.

   Example keyword triggers (will be refined during parsing):
   - Website Experience & Design: website, webpage, UX, user experience, navigation, interface, layout, mobile, responsive
   - AI / Automation-Driven Systems: AI, machine learning, algorithm, automation, recommender, personalization, bot, automated
   - Consumer / Trust Signals: trust, privacy, disclosure, reputation, review, rating, credibility, signal
   - Visual / Multimedia Interactions: image, video, visual, multimedia, AR, VR, infographic, visualisation, visualization

5. Build the Markdown output:
   - Top-level header with metadata: date, total rows parsed, total unique entries (if dedupe), classification method used, and counts per topic.
   - For each topic heading, include a one-line summary of the topic and the keyword triggers used.
   - Under each topic, list entries as bullets with the format:
     - Title (linked to preferred URL) — Authors. Journal (Year). DOI: <link if present>
       - Notes: <abstract highlights / scraped notes / PDF URL>
   - If an entry belongs to multiple topics, include it under each relevant heading but add a parenthetical note "(also listed under: ... )". Optionally include a single canonical list in an Appendix that shows each paper once with a source file tag.

6. Verification and checks:
   - Report raw row counts per XML file and sum.
   - Report number of Markdown entries (count bullets) and ensure it equals the expected count (either raw sum or unique count depending on dedupe choice). If mismatched, produce a diagnostic table of missing rows.
   - Spot-check by showing 5 random entries and the keywords that triggered their assigned topic(s).

# Deliverables
- `organized-scholar-entries.md` written to /Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/
- A short verification block at the top of the Markdown file with counts and a note on deduplication/classification choices.
- This plan file updated with the exact keyword triggers used (so classification is repeatable).

# Risks & mitigations
- Excel-XML structural variations: if worksheet names or column names differ, parser will fallback to reading the first worksheet and mapping columns by header text. I will surface any unexpected column names.
- Missing DOIs: use PDF URL or OA link when DOI absent; surface missing DOI count in verification.

# Next step / Clarifying questions
I will proceed once you confirm two choices below:
1) Which classification method do you want? (Use suggested 4 topics or auto-extract clusters?)
2) Should I deduplicate identical entries across exports (dedupe by DOI/title) or preserve all exported rows as-is?

Once you answer, I will exit plan mode and run the parsing & generation steps.


# Repeatable keyword triggers (initial draft)
- Website Experience & Design: website, webpage, ux, "user experience", navigation, interface, layout, mobile, responsive, homepage, design
- AI / Automation-Driven Systems: ai, "machine learning", algorithm, automation, recommender, personalization, bot, "automated", nlp, "deep learning"
- Consumer / Trust Signals: trust, privacy, disclosure, reputation, review, rating, credibility, signal, "trustworthy", "information disclosure"
- Visual / Multimedia Interactions: image, video, visual, multimedia, ar, vr, augmented, 3d, infographic, visualisation, visualization

content.content: The file /Users/my/.claude/plans/happy-cooking-puzzle.md has been updated successfully.

+   - AI / Automation-Driven Systems: AI, machine learning, algorithm, automation, recommender, personalization, bot, automated
+   - Consumer / Trust Signals: trust, privacy, disclosure, reputation, review, rating, credibility, signal
+   - Visual / Multimedia Interactions: image, video, visual, multimedia, AR, VR, infographic, visualisation, visualization
+5. Build the Markdown output:
+   - Top-level header with metadata: date, total rows parsed, total unique entries (if dedupe), classification method used, and counts per topic.
+   - For each topic heading, include a one-line summary of the topic and the keyword triggers used.
+   - Under each topic, list entries as bullets with the format:
+     - Title (linked to preferred URL) — Authors. Journal (Year). DOI: <link if present>
+       - Notes: <abstract highlights / scraped notes / PDF URL>

+   - If an entry belongs to multiple topics, include it under each relevant heading but add a parenthetical note "(also listed under: ... )". Optionally include a single canonical list in an Appendix that shows each paper once with a source file tag.
+6. Verification and checks:
+   - Report raw row counts per XML file and sum.

+   - Report number of Markdown entries (count bullets) and ensure it equals the expected count (either raw sum or unique count depending on dedupe choice). If mismatched, produce a diagnostic table of missing rows.
+   - Spot-check by showing 5 random entries and the keywords that triggered their assigned topic(s).
+# Deliverables
+- `organized-scholar-entries.md` written to /Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/
+- A short verification block at the top of the Markdown file with counts and a note on deduplication/classification choices.
+- This plan file updated with the exact keyword triggers used (so classification is repeatable).
+# Risks & mitigations

+- Excel-XML structural variations: if worksheet names or column names differ, parser will fallback to reading the first worksheet and mapping columns by header text. I will surface any unexpected column names.
+- Missing DOIs: use PDF URL or OA link when DOI absent; surface missing DOI count in verification.
+# Next step / Clarifying questions
+I will proceed once you confirm two choices below:
+1) Which classification method do you want? (Use suggested 4 topics or auto-extract clusters?)
+2) Should I deduplicate identical entries across exports (dedupe by DOI/title) or preserve all exported rows as-is?
+Once you answer, I will exit plan mode and run the parsing & generation steps.
+# Repeatable keyword triggers (initial draft)
+- Website Experience & Design: website, webpage, ux, "user experience", navigation, interface, layout, mobile, responsive, homepage, design
+- AI / Automation-Driven Systems: ai, "machine learning", algorithm, automation, recommender, personalization, bot, "automated", nlp, "deep learning"
+- Consumer / Trust Signals: trust, privacy, disclosure, reputation, review, rating, credibility, signal, "trustworthy", "information disclosure"
+- Visual / Multimedia Interactions: image, video, visual, multimedia, ar, vr, augmented, 3d, infographic, visualisation, visualization

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Context
User has four Google Scholar export XML files (MISQ, JMIS, ISR, MS) that capture bibliographic metadata scraped from web searches. They want a single Markdown summary that preserves every citation and organizes them by research topic (per their preference for theme-based grouping).

# Approach
1. Parse each Excel-style XML file (e.g., `Scholar Export` worksheet) to extract every row’s Title, Authors, Journal, Year, Abstract/Notes, DOI/Open access URL, and PDF URL so no paper is missed.
2. Review titles/abstract notes to identify a small set of consistent research directions (e.g., Website Experience & Design, AI/Automation-Driven Systems, Consumer/Trust Signals, Visual/Multimedia Interactions). Record which keywords or concepts trigger each category so the classification logic can be repeated if more exports arrive.
3. Draft a Markdown summary (`organized-scholar-entries.md` in the project folder) with a top-level heading for each topic. Under each heading, list every relevant citation as a bullet containing the title (linked to DOI/PDF if available), authors, journal/year, and any salient notes (e.g., abstract highlights). Maintain the original count of entries by cross-checking totals against each XML file.
4. Double-check that no rows were skipped (compare row totals across files and the Markdown entry counts) and that every provided metadata field is surfaced in the summary.

# Files to Modify
- `/Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/scholar-export-2026-03-20 MISQ.xml` (read-only data source)
- `/Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/scholar-export-2026-03-20 JMIS.xml` (read-only data source)
- `/Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/scholar-export-2026-03-20 ISR.xml` (read-only data source)
- `/Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/scholar-export-2026-03-20 MS.xml` (read-only data source)
- `/Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/organized-scholar-entries.md` (new summary output)

# Verification
- Confirm the number of clipped citations in the Markdown file matches the total number of rows across the four XML exports.
- Ensure each entry includes at least title, authors, journal/year, and a hyperlink to the DOI or PDF URL.
- Validate that every topic heading contains at least one paper, and no citation appears under the wrong theme (spot-check by matching keywords to categories).

input.plan:
# Context
User has four Google Scholar export XML files (MISQ, JMIS, ISR, MS) that capture bibliographic metadata scraped from web searches. They want a single Markdown summary that preserves every citation and organizes them by research topic (theme-based grouping).

# Goal
Produce `/Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/organized-scholar-entries.md` containing every citation from the four XML exports, grouped under clear research-topic headings, with each entry including:
- Title (linked to DOI or PDF when available)
- Authors
- Journal / Year
- Any abstract/notes or salient highlights
- DOI / open-access URL / PDF URL surfaced explicitly

Also produce a simple classification ruleset (keywords -> topic) saved inside this plan file for repeatability.

# Files (read-only sources)
- /Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/scholar-export-2026-03-20 MISQ.xml
- /Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/scholar-export-2026-03-20 JMIS.xml
- /Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/scholar-export-2026-03-20 ISR.xml
- /Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/scholar-export-2026-03-20 MS.xml

# Output (to create)
- /Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/organized-scholar-entries.md (Markdown summary)

# Constraints and non-negotiables
- Do NOT modify the source XML files (read-only).
- Preserve every row unless the user requests deduplication (see question below).
- Maintain the total-entry count and report it in the Markdown header.
- All generated outputs and verification logs will be stored in the project folder (no changes to `papers/`).

# Proposed approach (implementation plan)
1. Parse the four XML files as spreadsheet-style XML (Excel "XML Spreadsheet 2003" format that Google Scholar provides). For each file, extract rows from the worksheet (expected worksheet name: "Scholar Export" or the first worksheet) and capture these fields (if present): Title, Authors, Journal, Year, Abstract/Notes, DOI/Open access URL, PDF URL, and any other columns.

2. Normalize metadata for each row:
   - Trim whitespace and unify whitespace/newlines in abstract/notes.
   - Normalize authors as provided (do not reformat names unless requested).
   - Prefer DOI link when present; otherwise use PDF URL or open-access URL.

3. (Optional) Deduplication: if requested, deduplicate across the four files using DOI when present, else normalized title string. By default, keep all rows and report duplicates separately.

4. Topic classification: two options (choose by user):
   A. Use the user's suggested topic set (recommended):
      - Website Experience & Design
      - AI / Automation-Driven Systems
      - Consumer / Trust Signals
      - Visual / Multimedia Interactions
      For each topic, classification triggers are keyword matches in Title + Abstract/Notes. I will produce a keyword list for each topic (examples below) and apply a simple multi-label assignment (a paper can appear under multiple topics). If a paper matches no keywords, it will go under "Other / Uncategorized".

   B. Auto-extract topics: use unsupervised clustering on title/abstract text (TF-IDF + Agglomerative or KMeans) to produce 4-6 coherent clusters, then label them manually. This is more flexible but harder to reproduce by simple keyword rules.

   Example keyword triggers (will be refined during parsing):
   - Website Experience & Design: website, webpage, UX, user experience, navigation, interface, layout, mobile, responsive
   - AI / Automation-Driven Systems: AI, machine learning, algorithm, automation, recommender, personalization, bot, automated
   - Consumer / Trust Signals: trust, privacy, disclosure, reputation, review, rating, credibility, signal
   - Visual / Multimedia Interactions: image, video, visual, multimedia, AR, VR, infographic, visualisation, visualization

5. Build the Markdown output:
   - Top-level header with metadata: date, total rows parsed, total unique entries (if dedupe), classification method used, and counts per topic.
   - For each topic heading, include a one-line summary of the topic and the keyword triggers used.
   - Under each topic, list entries as bullets with the format:
     - Title (linked to preferred URL) — Authors. Journal (Year). DOI: <link if present>
       - Notes: <abstract highlights / scraped notes / PDF URL>
   - If an entry belongs to multiple topics, include it under each relevant heading but add a parenthetical note "(also listed under: ... )". Optionally include a single canonical list in an Appendix that shows each paper once with a source file tag.

6. Verification and checks:
   - Report raw row counts per XML file and sum.
   - Report number of Markdown entries (count bullets) and ensure it equals the expected count (either raw sum or unique count depending on dedupe choice). If mismatched, produce a diagnostic table of missing rows.
   - Spot-check by showing 5 random entries and the keywords that triggered their assigned topic(s).

# Deliverables
- `organized-scholar-entries.md` written to /Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/
- A short verification block at the top of the Markdown file with counts and a note on deduplication/classification choices.
- This plan file updated with the exact keyword triggers used (so classification is repeatable).

# Risks & mitigations
- Excel-XML structural variations: if worksheet names or column names differ, parser will fallback to reading the first worksheet and mapping columns by header text. I will surface any unexpected column names.
- Missing DOIs: use PDF URL or OA link when DOI absent; surface missing DOI count in verification.

# Next step / Clarifying questions
I will proceed once you confirm two choices below:
1) Which classification method do you want? (Use suggested 4 topics or auto-extract clusters?)
2) Should I deduplicate identical entries across exports (dedupe by DOI/title) or preserve all exported rows as-is?

Once you answer, I will exit plan mode and run the parsing & generation steps.


# Repeatable keyword triggers (initial draft)
- Website Experience & Design: website, webpage, ux, "user experience", navigation, interface, layout, mobile, responsive, homepage, design
- AI / Automation-Driven Systems: ai, "machine learning", algorithm, automation, recommender, personalization, bot, "automated", nlp, "deep learning"
- Consumer / Trust Signals: trust, privacy, disclosure, reputation, review, rating, credibility, signal, "trustworthy", "information disclosure"
- Visual / Multimedia Interactions: image, video, visual, multimedia, ar, vr, augmented, 3d, infographic, visualisation, visualization
